hypothesis: States must implement targeted measures to address gender-based violence, which costs the EU economy an estimated 289 billion euros annually.
All EU budget programmes must be implemented taking into account the principle of gender equality through an appropriate gender mainstreaming methodology.
Employers must apply the principle of equal pay for equal work to close the gender pay gap, currently at approximately 16% across EU Member States.
Member States must take effective measures to enforce the legal principle of equal pay for equal work and improve pay transparency at the national level.
Employers must adopt gender-neutral job evaluation methods and promote gender-balanced representation in managerial positions to reduce occupational segregation.
Relevant authorities must regularly publish disaggregated data on the gender pay gap, its determinants, and its consequences on earnings and pensions.
The European Commission must adopt a stand-alone gender equality strategy that includes specific policies aimed at closing the gender pay gap.
Gender equality policies are vital to economic growth, and organisations must promote the equal participation of women and men in decision-making at all levels.
Member States must strive to at least halve the gender employment gap compared to 2019 levels by 2030, as required by the European Pillar of Social Rights Action Plan.
Member States must ensure equal sharing of caring responsibilities between women and men through adequate parental, paternity, and carers' leave provisions.
Listed companies must introduce measures to encourage career progression for women at all levels of management and address the underrepresentation of women on boards.
Each listed company must develop and implement a gender equality policy covering recruitment practices, mentoring schemes, and career development guidance for women.
Listed companies must ensure that members of the underrepresented sex hold at least 40% of non-executive director positions by 30 June 2026.
Listed companies must apply gender-balance criteria at every appropriate stage of the director selection and shortlisting process.
Listed companies must publish annually on their website and in their annual reports the gender composition of their boards and the measures taken to achieve gender balance.
Listed companies must achieve a more balanced representation of women and men among their directors through effective and legally binding measures.
Member States must ensure that listed companies achieve either 40% representation of the underrepresented sex among non-executive directors or 33% across all director positions by 30 June 2026.
A Member State may suspend the gender balance obligation under this Directive only if the underrepresented sex already holds at least 30% of non-executive director positions.
Member States must submit to the European Commission a comprehensive report on the implementation of the Women on Boards Directive by 29 December 2025 and every two years thereafter.
By 31 December 2030, the Commission must review and assess whether the Directive has effectively improved gender balance on boards and consider extending its scope if needed.
EU external action must combine gender mainstreaming, targeted actions, and political dialogue to accelerate progress towards global gender equality and empowerment goals.
The Horizon Europe programme must support gender studies, women's access to research funding, and the integration of a gender perspective in research content.
EU Delegations must prepare a country-level implementation plan integrating gender equality priorities and ensuring meaningful participation of women's rights organisations.
The EU must actively promote the inclusion of gender-responsive language in all multilateral resolutions and declarations on gender equality and women's rights.
All persons have the right to live free from gender-based violence, and states must fund programmes to end child marriage and female genital mutilation globally.
EU humanitarian assistance must include systematic action to prevent and respond to sexual and gender-based violence from the very onset of any humanitarian crisis.
EU external action must support legislation criminalising all forms of gender-based violence and strengthen law enforcement capacity through a victim-centred approach.
EU funding must support the elimination of harmful practices such as female genital mutilation and child marriage while ensuring access to sexual and reproductive health services.
Legal frameworks must be reformed to eliminate discriminatory regulations on property ownership, access to bank credit, and inheritance rights that disadvantage women.
States must ensure sustained funding of universal health coverage, including sexual and reproductive health services, with particular attention to women and girls with disabilities.
EU external action must promote universal health coverage by ensuring equitable access to essential health services and information, including for women and girls with disabilities.
Women and girls must participate equally in public and private spheres, including as elected officials, peace negotiators, and mediators, to fulfil the right to democracy for all.
States and organisations must address structural barriers causing the digital gender divide, ensuring women's equal access to digital technologies and ICT career opportunities.
EU institutions must achieve gender parity of 50/50 in management positions and embed gender equality commitments in leadership appointment and performance assessment criteria.
The European Commission must publish annual progress reports on GAP III implementation and conduct a mid-term and final evaluation of its impact on gender equality.
The European Parliament and the Council must endorse the EU Gender Action Plan III and cooperate with the Commission in its full implementation.
States parties must protect women from all forms of violence and establish comprehensive policies and support measures for all victims of domestic violence.
The Convention applies to all forms of violence against women, including domestic violence, in both peacetime and situations of armed conflict.
States parties must adopt legislative and other measures to promote and protect the right of all individuals, especially women, to live free from violence in public and private life.
States must exercise due diligence to prevent, investigate, and prosecute acts of violence against women committed by non-state actors and ensure compensation for victims.
States parties must establish effective and coordinated national policies to prevent and combat all forms of gender-based violence, placing the rights of victims at the centre of all measures.
States parties must recognise, encourage, and support relevant NGOs and civil society organisations working to combat violence against women at all levels.
States parties must include in school curricula at all levels educational materials on gender equality, non-stereotyped gender roles, and the right to personal integrity.
States parties must establish or support intervention programmes for domestic violence perpetrators aimed at encouraging non-violent behaviour and preventing future offences.
States parties must ensure adequate cooperation mechanisms among all relevant authorities to protect and support victims of violence, prioritising their safety and human rights.
States parties must ensure that victims of violence have access to information about available regional and international individual and collective complaint mechanisms.
States parties must provide age-appropriate psychosocial support to child witnesses of violence and ensure their rights and needs are considered in all victim protection services.
States parties must encourage and facilitate the reporting of gender-based violence by any witness to competent authorities or relevant organisations.
States parties must guarantee victims of gender-based violence the right to claim compensation from perpetrators and provide state compensation where other sources are unavailable.
States parties must criminalise intentional conduct that seriously impairs the psychological integrity of a person through coercion or threats.
States parties must criminalise stalking, defined as intentional and repeated threatening conduct directed at another person that causes them to fear for their safety.
States parties must criminalise all forms of female genital mutilation, including excision and infibulation, and all acts of inciting or compelling a woman or girl to undergo such procedures.
States parties must ensure that offences under the Convention are punishable with effective, proportionate, and dissuasive sanctions, including imprisonment where appropriate.

Competent authorities must have the power to order domestic violence perpetrators to immediately leave the victim's residence and prohibit them from approaching the victim in situations of imminent danger.

States parties must ensure that the statute of limitations for serious sexual offences is extended sufficiently to allow victims who were minors at the time to prosecute perpetrators after reaching adulthood.
States parties must promptly inform requesting states of the outcome of requested actions and may spontaneously share information to help prevent or investigate offences under the Convention.
